qualitative.summary: Captures major workflow steps (create study, set title, configure research method, set location, add screener questions, submit) with reasonable fidelity, but misses many granular interactions (hover events, loading transitions, hesitations) and has moderate timing offsets of 1-6s on matched events.

issues.description: Baseline contains ~42 hover events; experiment captures only 4. Cursor movements over study list items, menu options, and cards are systematically omitted.
examples: Baseline has ~15 hover events over study list items, menu options, and selection cards. Experiment has only 3-4 hover events total.

issues.description: Baseline records ~40 change_ui_state events (loading spinners, step transitions). Experiment captures only 2. Most loading transitions between wizard steps are absent.
examples: Baseline records loading transitions between every wizard step; experiment skips all of these.

issues.description: Key actions have timing offsets of 1-6s compared to baseline, likely due to frame-based detection.
examples: Click +Create button: experiment 530238ms vs baseline 530588ms (350ms offset)

issues.description: Experiment produces 3 dwell events with no baseline counterpart. Baseline uses no dwell type.
examples: 3 dwell events generated where baseline has hover or no event

examples: Scroll on Quota page at 615821ms not in baseline

issues.description: Minor type disagreements where experiment uses click and baseline uses select for same action.

examples: Dense cluster of scrolls in results page where baseline has 2-3 scrolls

break_decision.explanation: This is the last (or only remaining) session.

systematic_patterns: change_ui_state near-total loss: ~5% recall across all sessions. Loading transitions, dropdown opens, tooltip appearances systematically missed.
Hover under-detection: Pipeline captures 10-30% of baseline hover events. Cursor-only movements without visual changes are invisible.
Dwell hallucination: Experiment generates dwell events in all sessions despite zero dwells in any baseline. Partially replaces hover/hesitate but with wrong semantics.
Click under-detection: Key button clicks (Search, Submit, CONTINUE, SELECT) frequently missed even when visual change occurs. Pipeline detects the state change but not the triggering click.
Input text blindness: Text input events missed in 8 of 12 sessions. Pipeline does not reliably detect typing activity.
Navigation under-detection: Page transitions captured at 17-71% rate. Within-flow navigations (click result -> navigate to page) particularly affected.
Scroll detection bias: Scrolls disproportionately well-detected relative to other types in some sessions, over-segmented in others. Flow-based detection over-triggers.
Token budget underutilization: All sessions use only 33-43% of available token budget, suggesting the observation pipeline is too conservative.

Cursor detection bottleneck: Detection rates range 3.7-100% across sessions. Low rates directly correlate with poor event coverage, but high rates (travel_expert_lisa: 100%) still yield poor results (F1=0.020).
Temporal coverage gaps: All sessions show significant gaps (20-137s) during active user interaction, indicating the moment selection algorithm misses extended interaction sequences.
judgment.root_cause_analysis:
Three pipeline stages contribute to the low F1=0.124, with the Observe and Analyse stages as co-primary bottlenecks and Merge as a minor factor.

**Observe stage (primary)**: Cursor template matching detection rates (3.7-100%) directly limit interaction event coverage. When cursor detection fails, the pipeline cannot identify interaction moments, leading to temporal gaps. However, cursor detection alone is insufficient -- travel_expert_lisa has 100% detection but F1=0.020, proving the Analyse stage is also a bottleneck. The token budget is consistently underutilized (33-43%), meaning the pipeline has capacity to process more moments but the moment selection algorithm is too conservative. Scene change detection works well but misses fine-grained interactions.

**Analyse stage (co-primary)**: Even with good observation data, the LLM extraction under-extracts events. Sessions with 60-168 moments produce only 14-116 events. The prompt (visual_change_driven.txt) focuses on visual changes rather than user intent, leading to: (1) change_ui_state events being described as context rather than extracted, (2) click events being subsumed into their visual consequences, (3) input_text events being missed when the typing is gradual, (4) dwell events being hallucinated as a catch-all for unclassified visual pauses.

**Merge stage (minor)**: time_tolerance_ms=2000 and similarity_threshold=0.7 appear reasonable. Some legitimate events may be lost to deduplication but this is not the primary issue.
strengths: Correct temporal ordering: Events that are detected maintain correct chronological sequence.
Reasonable timing accuracy: Matched events have mean timing offsets of 858-1229ms, well within the 2000ms tolerance.
Multi-site journey detection: Sessions crossing multiple websites (cfs_home_loan_sasha, travel_expert_veronika) have correct site-to-site transitions.
Form interaction detection: When detected, form fills and selections have reasonable accuracy (cfs_home_loan_sasha Unloan application flow captured well).
Scene change detection: Major page loads and navigation transitions reliably trigger visual change detection.
weaknesses: Catastrophically low recall (0.082): Pipeline misses 92% of ground truth events, rendering output insufficient for production use.
Cursor detection unreliability: Template matching approach fails on many cursor styles, limiting the entire pipeline.
Event type vocabulary mismatch: Pipeline generates dwell events not in any baseline; misses hesitate events entirely.
Token budget waste: 57-67% of LLM token budget goes unused, representing lost extraction capacity.
No input_text detection in most sessions: Typing is one of the most important interaction types but is unreliably detected.
Observer/participant confusion: Dual-screen sessions (travel_learner_sophia_jayde) cause the pipeline to track the wrong screen.

recommendations.rationale: Cursor tracking should remain enabled but needs fundamental improvement. Current template matching approach (3.7-100% detection range) is the primary bottleneck. Consider adding ML-based cursor detection or larger template sets. This is not a config change but a pipeline improvement.

The Analyse stage under-extracts events even with good observation data (travel_expert_lisa: 100% cursor detection, F1=0.020). A more capable model may better identify interaction events from visual moments. This is the highest-impact single config change.
All sessions use only 33-43% of the 50k token budget. Increasing to 80k allows more moments to be processed per minute, potentially capturing interactions in current temporal gaps.

Many missed events involve small UI changes (dropdown options, button state changes, text cursor movement) that may fall below the 500px minimum area threshold. Lowering to 300px would capture smaller visual changes.

Temporal coverage gaps of 20-137s suggest the pipeline needs more frequent baseline sampling during periods without detected visual changes. Reducing from 5s to 3s would catch more gradual interactions.
